completeness.rationale: All seven expected items were captured: ~5,000 tasks, slow on description/fast on title, v2.2→v2.3 regression, started ~2 weeks ago, 10-15 second latency, CPU spike (reporter volunteered fan noise, agent correctly interpreted as CPU-bound), and long descriptions with pasted meeting notes. The summary also surfaced additional useful signals: no caching improvement on repeated searches, and the constant-time behavior regardless of result count.

accuracy.rationale: The root cause hypothesis precisely matches both acceptable diagnostic paths simultaneously: it correctly identifies the v2.2→v2.3 upgrade as the trigger and correctly identifies the title-vs-description split as evidence of an indexing regression. The agent explicitly named LIKE '%term%' as a candidate implementation — which is exactly what happened. The reasoning chain (constant time regardless of match count → full table scan; CPU spike → row-by-row computation; no caching benefit → not a warm-cache path) is technically sound and leads directly to the actual cause without any material inaccuracies.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent asked all the high-value diagnostic questions and even designed an active test to confirm the full-scan hypothesis. The main gaps — exact OS/hardware specs, whether the reporter checked v2.3 release notes, and whether they tried reindexing or cache clearing — were all noted as information gaps in the summary rather than surfaced through questioning. These are secondary, but leaving them unasked is a minor shortcoming.

economy.rationale: Three agent turns, each extracting distinct and non-overlapping information. Turn 1 established timeline and scale. Turn 2 differentiated search types and tested for caching. Turn 3 ran a targeted diagnostic test. No question was repeated or redundant. The conversation reached resolution with minimal overhead.

question_quality.rationale: The questions show genuine diagnostic reasoning rather than generic checklists. Turn 2's question about 'consistent vs faster on a second search' probed the caching hypothesis — information the reporter would never have volunteered. Turn 3 designed a controlled experiment (rare term vs common term) to distinguish between a result-processing bottleneck and a full-table-scan bottleneck. This is the kind of question that separates a skilled diagnostician from a form-filler.

actionability.rationale: The summary gives a developer an immediate path: diff the description search implementation between v2.2 and v2.3, run EXPLAIN/query plan to verify index usage, restore the index or revert the implementation if changed. The proposed test case is specific and verifiable. Reproduction steps are concrete. A developer could open the v2.2 and v2.3 diffs and know exactly what to look for.

notable_strengths: The controlled diagnostic test in turn 3 (rare term vs common term, both taking the same time) was a high-signal move that transformed a plausible hypothesis into a near-confirmed finding before resolution

The reasoning chain in the root cause hypothesis is rigorous: four independent evidence points each independently point to full-table-scan, and the agent articulated why each one rules out alternative explanations
The agent correctly identified both acceptable diagnostic paths simultaneously rather than choosing one, which makes the summary robust to the actual implementation details
notable_weaknesses: OS and hardware specs were left as an information gap rather than asked directly — minor, but the environment section is incomplete as a result
The agent did not ask whether the reporter had tried any workarounds (reindexing, title-only search) or checked v2.3 release notes, both of which could have added useful context
most_insightful_question: Search for a unique term appearing in only 1-2 tasks vs a common term appearing in hundreds — if both take the same time, that confirms the bottleneck is scanning all rows rather than processing results, pointing squarely at a missing index.
missed_opportunities: Ask the reporter to check the v2.3 changelog or release notes for any mention of search changes
Ask whether they had tried reindexing or any other workaround
Ask for exact OS and hardware specs (ThinkPad T14, Ubuntu 22.04 were known but not captured)
